title: Incident reviews without the theater
hook: A postmortem is not a courtroom. It is a debugging session for the system that made the incident possible.

beats.heading: The first story is usually too simple

blocks.html: Right after an incident, everyone wants the clean sentence: deploy broke checkout. Human clicked wrong button. Alert was missed. Those may be true, but they are rarely enough. The useful question is: why did that action make sense at the time?

blocks.title: incident · rough timeline
blocks.caption: A timeline is evidence, not a blame map.
Leadership bugs are system bugs too. Pull context out and the team graph starts wobbling.

blocks.html: Blameless means you investigate without making people hide details to survive the meeting. It does not mean shrugging. You can be kind to people and ruthless about weak signals, risky defaults, confusing tools, and missing guardrails.

blocks.text: Replace "who caused this?" with "what made this easy to do and hard to catch?"

blocks.html: Training everyone to be more careful is usually a weak action item. Stronger actions alter the path: add an automated check, change the deploy order, page the right owner, make rollback one command, or remove the confusing option.

blocks.code:
- Add migration smoke test before production deploy
- Move payment errors to paging alert, owner: infra
- Add checkout rollback runbook with one-command path
- Delete stale processor config after audit
blocks.caption: Good action items are specific enough to close.

blocks.title: team clarity switchboard
blocks.caption: Flip the switches and watch the story turn into a tiny operating model.
blocks.low: muddy: people can move, but they are guessing while they do it
blocks.mid: clearer: ownership and tradeoffs are starting to show up
blocks.high: aligned: the team can act without decoding hidden context

beats.heading: A review should be useful six months later

blocks.html: Future engineers will not remember the meeting. They will find the doc during a similar outage. Write for them: impact, timeline, contributing factors, detection gaps, what changed, and how to tell if it worked.
